articles.title: THIS IS AN INTERVENTION: YOU'RE POSTING TOO MUCH
articles.summary: Platforms are fragmenting, but spamming volume won't help you squeeze more out of social. If anything, that extra content creates drag, not momentum.

articles.title: PINTEREST PARENTING TREND REPORT 2026: RAISING SCREEN‑SMART KIDS WHO SEEK REAL-WORLD ADVENTURE
articles.summary: Parents are embracing experience-rich, screen-smart childhoods. Pinterest searches for “screen free activities” are up 200% and “no phone summer” up 340%. Families are blending screens into shared activities like movie nights and sports. They're rethinking parenting with slower, kinder approaches and using nostalgia, DIY projects, and creative home setups to enrich daily life.

articles.title: WHY DO WE STILL CALL THEM PODCASTS?
articles.summary: Video podcasts are reshaping the medium and raising questions about whether the term “podcast” still fits. Once defined as on-demand audio shows, podcasts now include episodic video content. Most audiences mix listening and watching, with only 7% exclusively watching and 13% exclusively listening. Video podcasts are expanding into formats traditionally dominated by TV, blending performance, storytelling, and social media elements. Even so, the word “podcast” continues to stick, even as the format evolves.

articles.title: WHY LIFE MOMENTS, NOT DEMOGRAPHICS, SHOULD DRIVE YOUR TARGETING STRATEGY
articles.summary: Many brands focus on age and income, but purchase decisions are driven by life events like marriage, relocation, parenthood, or career changes. Consumers with the same demographics can have opposite intent, making broad-segment messaging and traditional loyalty models less effective. Strategies that combine life event signals with behavioral data and optimize for lifetime value across stages perform better.

articles.title: CONTENT STRATEGY THAT BUILDS PIPELINE: DISCOVERY, VIRALITY, AND RELATIONSHIP LOOPS (43 MINUTE PODCAST)
articles.summary: B2B content often produces lots of output but little impact because the pieces aren't connected. This podcast shares a four-part framework that can fix this. Content IP focuses on naming the buyer's real, often unspoken problem to build trust. Discovery loops make sure prospects can find your content through search, competitor comparisons, and product pages. Virality loops keep content visible by having real people consistently share stories, not just brand accounts posting updates. Relationship loops use recurring touchpoints like newsletters, webinars, or podcasts to deepen trust over time. When all four work together, content shifts from a task list into a growth engine.

articles.title: DESIGNING EXPERIMENTS FOR LONG-TERM GROWTH (WEBINAR)
articles.summary: This webinar will discuss experiment best practices and common mistakes that can lead to critical misinformation. The marketing team at GrowthBook will be sharing real examples of experimentation gone wrong and done right. It will take place on March 3 at 12 PM ET.

articles.title: STUDY: HOW ANSWERING PEOPLE ALSO ASK QUESTIONS CORRELATES WITH ORGANIC RANKINGS
articles.summary: Pages that fully answer more People Also Ask questions tend to rank higher in the top 5 organic results. This study analyzed 563 keywords and found the strongest correlations for commercial and transactional queries, such as a top 5 correlation of -0.9573 for transactional searches. Informational and navigational queries showed weaker relationships, and the correlation dropped sharply beyond page 1. Answering PAA questions alone does not guarantee rankings because technical quality, external signals, and user experience still matter. Broad question coverage can make content semantically richer and may support stronger ranking potential.

articles.title: SHOULD I QUIT MY JOB? A FLOWCHART
articles.summary: Going freelance is appealing, but it requires honest self checks. The first step is to confirm you truly want independence rather than simply wanting a better version of your current job. Early validation matters, so having at least one paying client before quitting can reduce risk. Expect about 20% of your time to go to admin work and about 30% to pipeline building, which leaves roughly 50% for the craft itself. Most new freelancers also need 3-6 months of savings and a clear specialty.

articles.title: PRODUCTS ARE EVALUATED TWICE NOW
articles.summary: Products now face two separate evaluations during buying decisions, relevance first and feasibility second. Awareness content still drives interest, but buyers often research technical fit on their own using AI and cross-functional review before contacting sales. Many teams fail because key answers live in operational documents that are not structured for buyers who are still deciding. Teams should map buyer roles to the depth of context they need and design intentionally gated materials that connect marketing to technical validation. Companies that build clear bridges between attention and fit will reduce friction and help serious buyers move faster.

articles.title: THE UNILEVER CEO HAS A NEW MARKETING DOCTRINE, AND IT IS COMPLETELY WRONG
articles.summary: Unilever's CEO has declared traditional advertising dead and shifted toward a “social-first, creator-led” approach. However, this ignores the realities of brand growth. Evidence shows big-brand, mass-reach campaigns remain essential for reaching new buyers, while creators are better at engaging existing fans. Applying a single strategy across 400 diverse brands repeats past mistakes and risks undermining brands that need broad reach.

articles.title: HOW TO MINE CUSTOMER PAID POINTS WITH CLAUDE
articles.summary: Use Claude to extract pain point questions from a customer interview, search them, and analyze top results in Semrush to uncover and expand relevant keywords.

articles.title: AI IS NOT IMPROVING PRODUCTIVITY
articles.summary: Right now, AI mostly replaces tasks and benefits large companies rather than workers.

articles.title: THE DISCOVER LIST: MEET THE 2026 TIKTOK CREATORS TO WATCH
articles.summary: 50 creators to watch across educators, foodies, icons, innovators, and originators.
